data.title: Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Core Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Summary

Broad U.S. equity markets experienced significant volatility in the second quarter, largely driven by the burgeoning trade war. Markets dropped over 10% in early April following the U.S. “Liberation Day” tariff announcements, then surged to new highs by late June as tariff concerns eased among investors. A momentum-driven headwind prevailed, driving lower-quality, higher-volatility stocks to generally outperform higher-quality, lower-volatility stocks—penalizing the kinds of stocks KAR focuses on, which offer the potential for growth with a smoother ride over the long term.

Fund Performance

The Virtus KAR Small-Mid Cap Core Fund returned 3.36% (MUTF:VKSIX) (Class I) in the quarter, underperforming the Russell 2500 TM Index, which returned 8.59%. Stock selection in the industrials and healthcare sectors detracted from performance, while underweights in the real estate and energy sectors contributed positively to performance.

Bentley Systems (BSY) and Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) were the largest contributors to performance during the quarter.

  • Bentley Systems is a software development company specializing in infrastructure engineering software. The company reported consistent profitable growth, particularly in software subscriptions, as the business benefits from a strengthening infrastructure spending environment.
  • Interactive Brokers, a global online brokerage firm, reported continued strong growth in client assets and number of client accounts, with continued strength in Asia and Europe. This strength in client growth drove growth in the company’s revenues and profits.

Charles River Laboratories (CRL) and Saia (SAIA) were the largest detractors from performance in the quarter.

  • Charles River, a research and drug development company, was impacted by a recent Food and Drug Administration (FDA) announcement about a planned shift from animal testing in medical research. We believe this creates a structural change in the company’s business model, and we sold our position.
  • Saia is a transportation company specializing in less-than-truckload (LTL) freight shipping. The company’s share price declined after it reported a decline in profits. Near-term profitability is being pressured by the added costs of new terminals opened last year and a still weak shipping environment, which may worsen over the coming months due to tariff uncertainty.

% Contribution: Absolute weighted contribution. To obtain the top/bottom holdings calculation methodology, call 800-243-4361.

Portfolio Changes

During the quarter, we purchased Hamilton Lane (HLNE) for the portfolio. We sold Charles River Laboratories (discussed above), Scotts Miracle-Gro (SMG), and Teradyne (TER).

  • Hamilton Lane is a holding company that engages in the provision of private markets investment solutions. The firm works with clients to conceive, structure, build out, manage, and monitor portfolios of private markets funds and direct investments. It also offers customized separate accounts, specialized funds, advisory services, distribution management, and reporting, monitoring, data, and analytics.
  • We sold Charles River in response to structural changes we believe impact its business. On April 10, the FDA announced plans to phase out animal testing for monoclonal antibodies and other drugs. Although the shift to non-animal testing is expected to be gradual and Charles River could benefit from artificial intelligence (AI)-based testing models it is developing, that time frame may be several years out.
  • We sold our shares in Scotts Miracle-Gro due to the lawn and garden care company’s lack of consistent capital allocation discipline. We became particularly concerned when Scotts announced the unexpected departure of its CFO. In view of the company’s large family ownership/board representation, we believe Scotts needs a strong independent voice that the current chair/CEO (son of the company’s founder) does not provide. When it became clear that the chief accounting officer serving as interim CFO was to be named to the permanent CFO role, that ruined our hopes for a strong replacement.
  • We sold our underweight position in Teradyne, a manufacturer of automated test equipment. We grew increasingly disappointed with the company’s capital allocation decisions particularly as it relates to its continuous investment in the inherently inferior, highly competitive industrial automation (robotics) business.

data.title: Masco Corporation: The Last Remaining Hurdle Is The DIY Segment
data.description:
Summary

Following my coverage on Masco Corp. (NYSE:MAS) in April, in which I recommended a hold rating as the consumer spending backdrop was getting worse, and to top it off, there were the headwinds in DIY and cost pressure, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock. There are good positive developments. Mainly, the tariff risks, which were once the biggest overhang, have eased, with MAS now confident in fully offsetting FY25 impacts through pricing and sourcing. Meanwhile, the pivot toward the Pro paint channel continues to show strong momentum. That said, DIY remains structurally weak, and until there is a clear recovery, my neutral stance on the stock remains intact.

Tariff Overhang Lifts

What was previously the single largest overhang on the MAS bull case was the significant and uncertain impact of tariffs, which, thankfully, has now cleared considerably. In my last write-up, I highlighted the risk from ~$400 million in tariff-related costs and management’s own admission of being in “uncharted territory” on passing those costs through. Fast forward to today, and the situation has improved dramatically. The estimated annualized cost from these tariffs has been revised down to a far more manageable $210 million. More importantly, for 2025, the in-year impact is now expected to be only $140 million, a figure that management confidently expects to fully offset through a combination of pricing, cost savings, and sourcing adjustments. This is a massive change in narrative vs. the last time I wrote an update on MAS, where it was expected to absorb a hit of up to $200 million. For perspective, this basically means the net tariff impact is $0 in FY25, and that $200 million is ~15% of FY24 EBIT. For the stock sentiment, this is a big positive, as it removes a major source of earnings volatility that was likely weighing on the stock.

An important factor that gives confidence MAS is able to fully offset that $140 million tariff impact in FY25 is the proven pricing power within the plumbing segment, which bears ~80% of the tariff impact. Referencing figures from the most recent quarter results (2Q25), this plumbing segment saw a 5% sales increase, of which 3% was driven by price increase. Management even went one step ahead to guide for a mid-single-digit price increase for FY25 for plumbing. From a mathematical standpoint, this implies that 2H25 will see a higher step-up in pricing vs. 1H25 (good for near-term visibility).

The pushback is whether MAS can really push this price increase through. I believe MAS can because of the product quality it sells. For example, in the Decorative segment, Behr was awarded the number one rating for interior paint for the 12th consecutive year, and MAS got all the top three spots with its Dynasty, Marquee, and Ultra products. In plumbing, Hansgrohe continues to win design awards for its products. Moreover, MAS is constantly innovating products to fit the demand requirements today. For reference, ~25% of MAS's total sales come from products launched within the last three years, and I think this is also a reason why customers are consistently choosing MAS because it has newer features and designs.

Furthermore, this pricing power is proven to be effective where it needs to be—the premium and luxury segments. Drawing readers back to the earnings call, management noted that the premium and luxury consumer cohort continues to spend and invest in remodeling and that higher-end brands are holding up better. This point is important because having a customer base that is willing to pay makes pushing price increases much easier; in other words, low price elasticity gives MAS more room to adjust pricing to offset costs without significantly impacting demand.

Pro Paint

Building on another bright spot I highlighted previously, MAS's pivot toward the professional paint channel is proving to be the right move. It is disappointing that the Do-It-Yourself [DIY] paint segment continues to struggle, weighed down by low existing home turnover and what management now frames as a more structural challenge tied to demographic shifts. But the pro-business side tells a very different story. Sales growth remains positive (mid-single-digits again in 2Q25), and I see clearer evidence that this momentum can continue.

The success with Home Depot (HD) is the main reason. I said before this improves MAS distribution reach, but what we have seen so far is it's more than that. This partnership has evolved to one that can help MAS capture more demand. Specifically, MAS and HD are now exploring programs such as trade credits for professional painters. I expect strong adoption here because this is a tool that directly addresses a core customer need of managing cash flow. If well executed, this should not only drive conversion but also strengthen long-term loyalty with business customers. Another growth angle from this partnership is that as HD pushes to capture more of the professional market through its SRS Distribution business, MAS should benefit from this as well. This is a big development because it means Behr’s Pro growth is no longer tied just to the success of HD retail stores; it can now tap into a massive, dedicated professional supply network, greatly expanding its addressable market.

Valuation

The last “hurdle” for MAS to clear before I will give a buy rating is in the Do-It-Yourself [DIY] segment, which remains as a material headwind for the Decorative Architectural segment. My previous concern that demographic shifts could weigh on DIY demand is still valid, and while management has ramped up efforts (like the recent introduction of ChatHUE, an AI tool to simplify the process of choosing a paint color), there is yet to be any recovery. It may be too early to determine these efforts as non-effective, but the reality is the DIY segment is still in negative growth. Remember that management also said DIY is a much bigger segment vs. Pro, so the simple math is that even with continued share gains in Pro, weakness of this magnitude in the larger DIY segment will limit the segment’s ability to materially drive earnings upside.

Hence, as bullish as I sounded above, this very last hurdle is something that is holding me back from upgrading to a buy. MAS valuation isn’t exactly cheap as well. Like before, this is still trading around the same level at ~16.7x forward PE. In fact, it has gone up one multiple turn. Perhaps the market is looking through this near-term housing weakness that is weighing on the paint DIY segment (especially since the Feds cut rates, which should drive down mortgages and hence push up housing turnover), but I think it's still too early to determine this will be the case. The historical trading range is ~14x to 19x forward PE (+/- 1 standard deviation), so just based on this, I think there is more downside skew. Overall, until DIY sees a recovery, I stand firm in my view not to upgrade to buy.

Conclusion

In my view, MAS has done a great job in addressing a key investor concern—tariffs—while also showing that it can drive growth in Pro Paint and Plumbing. These positives support earnings visibility and a lower risk profile. However, the persistent weakness in DIY remains the single largest hurdle preventing a re-rating. With the stock already trading at ~16.7x forward PE, I see limited near-term upside. Until there is tangible improvement in DIY demand, I continue to recommend a hold rating for MAS.

data.title: Volkswagen: Guidance Cut After P911 Profit Warning
data.description:
Today, we return to review Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTCPK:VWAPY) following Dr. Ing. h.c. F. Porsche AG (or P911) issued a profit warning last week. P911’s release stands out as an early warning signal for the broader auto sector, highlighting the challenges of the BEV transition and carrying direct implications for Volkswagen Group. Indeed, the company owns about 75.4% of Porsche AG. Today, after briefly reviewing the company’s Q2 results, we assess the impact on Volkswagen’s 2025 performance. Volkswagen’s share price has shown little movement since our previous update (Q1 results comment - Fig. 1). Here at the Lab, we have shifted our stance on P911 to a lower rating.

Fig 1

Q2 Results and P911 Profit Warning Impact

In Q2, the company reported a core operating profit margin of 4.7%. This was ahead of Wall Street analysts' consensus, and the beat came in despite tariff costs exceeding expectations. This upside was mainly related to solid performance in the mass-market division, which delivered a 6.3% operating margin in the Brand Group Core segment (Fig. 2). On a positive one-off, Volkswagen AG benefited from a one-off license fee payment from the Chinese joint ventures, worth about €500 million. At the aggregate level, the company's car deliveries were up by 1.3% to 4.4 million vehicles. Sales were broadly flat compared to last year at €158.4 billion, with an EBIT of €6.7 billion. This represents a 33% decline compared to H1 2024 results. The company was impacted by higher import tariffs and additional provisions related to the CO₂ fleet regulations in the EU. Volkswagen is also progressing with higher expenses for restructuring activities, and as a consequence, net income decreased by €2.8 billion to €4.5 billion. Post H1 results, Volkswagen lowered its 2025 guidance (Fig. 3).

Source: Volkswagen Q2 results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

With the new guidance now available, the earlier Q2 outlook has effectively been superseded. P911’s decision to overhaul its product strategy — notably by postponing future BEV launches indefinitely — triggered a one-off charge of €1.8 billion. At the Volkswagen AG level, the same measure has a €2.1 billion effect since Porsche’s move also impacts Audi. In addition, the company recorded a non-cash impairment of €3 billion on the P911 investment.

As a result, the company has revised (once again) its Fiscal Year 2025 operating margin guidance down to 2–3% from the previous 4–5% (Fig. 3). This 200 basis points reduction translates to roughly €6.5 billion in operating profit, which is greater than the combined €5.1 billion impairment. This difference comes from two additional factors built into the new outlook: 1) the Traton profit warning disclosed post-H1 results (which came in after VW’s Q2 release), and 2) the EU/US tariff agreement. These additional 10 basis points explain the gap. Still key to report is the fact that FCF guidance has been cut to around zero (from the earlier €1–€3 billion range).

Under the revised outlook, operating profit is expected to land in the €8–8.5 billion range, with top-line sales assumptions unchanged. Any potential benefit from lower future depreciation and amortization is still uncertain. Beyond Porsche’s challenges — proven more severe than many anticipated — the outlook for Brand Group Core and Progressive remains intact. Encouragingly, the €5.1 billion P911-related write-downs will be excluded from the dividend calculation, supporting the case for a DPS of roughly €5. Volkswagen’s already fragile FCF profile will likely face further strain from P911’s anticipated cash outflows in the coming years.

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Given the current uncertainty, producing our reliable forecast is challenging, so we rely on Volkswagen’s updated estimates. From a valuation standpoint, considering €6.5 billion in EBIT, we also include €1 billion in interest expenses and a corporate tax rate of 28.5%. This results in a net earnings of €3.2 billion. This represents an EPS of €6.4. This represents a P/E of >14x. Looking to 2026, if we normalized the results, applying a similar turnover with a 5.5% EBIT margin, our EPS would reach €25.3. While short-term dynamics justify a neutral stance on Volkswagen AG, our strategic horizon continues to warrant a buy rating. We find Volkswagen’s share price performance disappointing, especially considering the strength of brands like Lamborghini and Ducati. For consistency reasons, we still applied a 5x P/E on a 2026 EPS projection of €25.3, so our target price is set at €126.6 ($14.6 in ADR). This is calculated with the current outstanding shares number.

Risks

Downside risks include: 1) US tariffs volatility and additional negative implications, 2) execution risks on the company's ongoing restructuring activities, 3) inability to pass on higher costs to consumers, 4) CO2 compliance costs, 5) additional profits warmings from the company's main holdings (Traton and P911), 6) additional competition in China, 7) FX changes (approximately 7.7m units are sold outside the EU), 8) supply chain constraints, 9) higher interest rates and 10) overall macro growth and negative consumer sentiment.

Conclusion

Last time, Volkswagen maintained its outlook despite the mixed Q1 operating performance. The latest guidance downgrade raises questions about the reliability of management’s outlook. We continue to rate the company overweight, though we acknowledge that the present momentum is challenging. This is a moment where the adage ‘buy when you dislike it most’ applies more than ever.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: RH: Near-Term Outlook Has Gotten More Uncertain
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about RH (NYSE:RH) previously with a hold rating, as there wasn't any near-term catalyst that could drive up the stock valuation, not especially with the weak consumer spending backdrop and tariff risk. The 2Q25 print reinforced my view. On one hand, demand resilience, early traction in Europe, and supply chain reshoring show that RH is executing well. On the other, the heavy reliance on promotions, combined with execution delays and a tough housing backdrop, creates a more uncertain near-term path. As such, I still give a hold rating.

2Q25 earnings

RH 2Q25 net revenue grew 8.4% y/y to $899 million, in line with the 8-10% guided range. Both segments contributed. The core RH business grew 8.4% y/y to $847 million, while Waterworks grew 7.6% y/y to $52 million. It was also positive that RH managed to expand adj. gross margin by 79 bps y/y to 46%, and that translated well into operating leverage, driving operating margin up by 340 bps to 15.1%. This is despite absorbing a 170 bps drag from European expansion costs. As a result, RH reported adj. EPS of $2.93.

Positive takeaways

The main positive takeaway is the resilience of underlying demand. While reported revenue grew 8.4% y/y, demand comps accelerated 13.7% y/y and are up 21% on a two-year basis. To put that in context, note that the current US housing backdrop is the worst housing market over the past two decades. Yet, despite that, RH was able to drive this level of demand, which I take as a signal that the brand is taking share from competitors. As to why there is such a big gap between demand comps and reported revenue, the 5.4-point gap is due to tariffs and should close in 2H25 as orders placed get delivered. In other words, it's a timing issue and not a red flag to be worried about.

Another key positive for RH's long-term story is the progress in its international expansion, which is beginning to build real momentum. In the UK, RH England is meaningfully exceeding expectations. For perspective, gallery demand grew 76% y/y in 2Q25, which is a sharp acceleration from the 47% growth seen in 1Q25. This puts the location on track to generate ~$37 to $39 million of gallery demand for the year, alongside another $8 million online. The implication goes beyond just the numbers here.

The way I see this is, if RH is able to achieve these results in a remote countryside estate, just imagine how much stronger performance can be if it were opened in a major metropolitan market. And I think the September 5th opening of RH Paris validates this thesis. Per management's words, the debut was a very strong start, with foot traffic in its first six days surpassing the New York flagship, which is the highest-volume gallery in RH. With London and Milan scheduled to open in spring 2026, this could be an inflection point in Europe, one where RH is reaching the critical mass needed to support larger advertising investments and accelerate brand awareness across Europe. If all these go well, RH valuation should see good support as it further diversifies itself from the US and also opens up a new growth runway-one that management expects could ultimately double the size of the business over the next five to seven years (this includes the Middle East).

Finally, RH is also taking steps to de-risk its operations by addressing tariff exposure and supply chain dependency, which are the two main bear arguments. The good news is that RH is rapidly reducing its reliance on China, with receipts expected to fall from 16% in 1Q25 to just 2% by 4Q25. Importantly, vendor partners are absorbing a portion of the related tariff costs, which is a key reason why RH was able to protect margins. This debate should ease and go away over time as RH's reshoring strategy progresses. By the end of FY25, management expects 52% of upholstered furniture to be produced in its North Carolina factory, with 21% in Italy and 12% in Mexico. Altogether, 85% of upholstered furniture will be outside of China. This not only reduces geopolitical risk but also builds a more resilient supply chain, one that should safeguard long-term margin structure.

Macro Pressures Cloud Near-Term Outlook

On the other hand, what worries me is the broader promotional environment. I have discussed this before, but I think the situation is getting more serious. In the call, management noted that "the entire luxury furniture industry is on sale," and that leaning into discounts is necessary to remain competitive and win share in a difficult housing backdrop. This worries me, as this suggests the demand growth we have seen may not be entirely organic. While this is understandable as a tactical lever to sustain demand, the fact is, like I said before, aggressive discounts condition customers to expect markdowns, and that risks eroding the premium positioning that has historically been RH's greatest strength. In luxury retail, brand equity and pricing power are difficult to rebuild once diluted, and this is a dynamic that investors cannot ignore.

The worst part is that we don't know if this is the worst of it. Could the 40% off drop further to 70% off? Looking at the housing turnover situation, it is hard to say with confidence that retailers won't dip further into the promotion cookie jar to pick up demand. That is probably the worst case for RH, as it would signal a complete capitulation on its luxury brand strategy, forcing it to sacrifice its long-term pricing power. Such a move would make it extremely difficult to ever return to a less promotional stance without sacrificing sales and could structurally impair its growth runway and its ability to recapture its >20% operating margin targets in the future.

Sure. Well, let's just start, Michael, with, in this world of furniture, at the luxury end, it's all on sale. Okay? At the highest end, in the top design showrooms, interior designers, architects, all get 30% or 40% off. Latest earnings call (2Q25)

Execution delays are also worrisome, which adds more uncertainty to RH's near-term outlook. The Fall Interiors Sourcebook, historically a critical driver of demand, was delayed by a full eight weeks as management waited for tariff clarity before finalizing pricing. For context, last year's sourcebooks were in-home by early August, but this year they won't be fully delivered until late September. That shift is huge because it pushes back the timeline for consumers to read, digest, compare, and place orders. The quantified impact is ~$40 million of revenue pushed out of 3Q25 and into 4Q25 and 1Q26, which is a meaningful drag to near-term numbers. There is also the postponement of a major brand extension (mind you, this was noted by management as an effort to target the largest part of the market and potentially bigger than Modern) to now be slated for spring 2026 (basically a huge near-term catalyst is gone).

Put together, these delays remove key growth catalysts from the near-term horizon, leaving the earnings path more uncertain and heavily back-end weighted.

Valuation

RH is still trading near where it was in July (15.2x forward EBIT today vs. 14.5x in July). Unlike in July, I think the situation has turned into greater near-term uncertainty (due to promotions and execution delays) but modestly better mid-to-long-term growth potential (due to success in international expansion). If I had to say, I would imagine RH's multiple to trend down instead of this 0.7x improvement, as there is a much bigger risk that RH could print worse-off numbers in the next 1 or 2 quarters if the promotional environment gets worse. Let's also not forget that the near-term growth catalysts have been removed. So, for investors that are looking to buy RH, there really isn't much to look forward to except hoping promotional intensity gets better, which I don't think will go away anytime soon looking at the housing market situation in the US.

Conclusion

I reiterate my hold rating for RH. There are positive signs of resilient demand and credible long-term growth opportunity from the international expansion traction. However, the earnings profile over the next few quarters looks more uncertain than before, weighed down by promotions, tariffs, and delays in key growth initiatives. With valuation trending upwards and little near-term upside catalyst, I do not see an immediate reason for investors to get more aggressive here. For now, I still maintain a hold rating until visibility improves.

data.title: Rates Spark: Whimsical Data Clouds The Fed's Path
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder

US rates are also setting the tone in euro rates

US rates are in the driving seat as investors try to gauge the Fed's outlook. A set of positive data surprises – an upwardly revised final second-quarter GDP reading as well as benign jobless claims figures – nudged rates higher from the front end. The 2y Treasury rose above 3.65%, the highest since late August, while the 10y US Treasury yield is approaching 4.2% again. With the market already anticipating a benign PCE inflation reading, confirmation should not alter the outlook. The risk is rather for rates to move another notch higher should price dynamics remain hotter.

Looking ahead to next week, the US will likely continue to determine market direction with the official jobs report for September to be released. At this early stage, not many market observers have submitted their estimates, but what we see so far is anywhere from 20k to 80k, thus highlighting the 'lottery' these releases have become.

EUR rates have been dragged higher alongside US rates, led by the belly of the curve. A less aggressive Fed at least lessens the probability of the European Central Bank reacting to FX effects. While it fits our structurally still bearish view, domestically the survey data over the past week pointed in the other direction, if anything. What EUR rates will be looking for in next week’s data is whether the flash CPI leaves the ECB with room to act.

Friday’s events and market views

From the eurozone, we will see CPI expectations from the ECB survey, but given the stable inflation dynamics of late, we don't foresee any surprises. More interesting will be US data, whereby the core PCE deflator for August is expected to come in at 0.2% month-on-month, a notch lower than July's 0.3%. Other US releases include personal income and spending data and the final University of Michigan sentiment indices.

Italy has 5Y and 10Y BTPs scheduled for auctioning, together with a 10Y green BTP and 9Y CCTeu. The total amount is €8.75bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Penguin Solutions: Momentum Building Beyond The Noise
data.description:
When I first initiated coverage on Penguin Solutions, Inc. (NASDAQ:PENG), I argued that Penguin was one of the most overlooked AI infrastructure players in the market. At the time, the stock traded below 1x EV/Sales even though it had accelerating growth and a set of partnerships that gave it real leverage in enterprise AI. Three months later, the stock is up ~36%, and I still see more room to run.

What's Changed Since My Last Thesis

When I called Penguin a Buy in June, my conviction rested on three pillars: vertical integration around AI infrastructure, credible partnerships with Dell and SK Telecom, and valuation that sat far below peers. The latest numbers and filings have only added new layers that strengthen my case.

Let's consider the results from Q3. Bookings are scaling, and the company has added five new enterprise wins in Q3, including federal, biotech, and energy customers. Profitability is also ahead of schedule: non-GAAP EPS grew 25% year over year, and full-year EPS guidance was raised to $1.80. Most importantly, though, it looks like memory has stepped forward as a second growth engine. What I initially saw as a supportive segment has now become central, and it recorded 42% year-over-year growth and early traction in CXL-based products.

On the capital side, the balance sheet is cleaner than it was three months ago, thanks to refinancing, reduced leverage, and $736 million in cash and short-term investments. Additionally, the company is now fully redomiciled to Delaware, so any issues regarding governance are out of the way. However, I must mention the news that came in late August, the Form S-3ASR, which registers shares tied to SK Telecom's convertible preferreds. For now, I think that it creates headline risk, but it doesn't really change the company's cash position or execution.

All told, the pieces that made me bullish in June are intact, and now we have stronger earnings power and a second growth leg in memory. Next, we'll look at the segments.

What's The Forward Focus In Each Segment?

Advanced Computing - My Cornerstone With Channel Reach

Advanced Computing brought in $132 million in Q3 2025, down from $200 million in Q2 and ~9% lower than the same period last year. Now, most of that drop is related to the timing of a big hyperscale deployment that had already been booked, so it's not a demand issue. That's good. At the same time, management has been clear that revenue here will always look a bit lumpy, so I'm not surprised. What matters to me is that Penguin added five new customers, including federal, biotech, and energy wins. I must highlight that these are the kind of deployments that tend to expand over time.

Leading on from that, this is where the Dell partnership becomes important. Penguin is now training Dell's global sales teams to sell its ICE ClusterWare software and services alongside Dell hardware, which gives Penguin access to customers it wouldn't reach on its own and turns the product into something sticky once it's in place. The more I look at this, the clearer it is that Penguin is building a systems-level role in enterprise AI.

Integrated Memory - Momentum Backed By SK hynix

Revenue here hit $130 million, up 42% YoY and 24% from last quarter. Demand came from across computing, networking, and telco, and I think that shows how broad the need for high-bandwidth, reliable memory has become in AI workloads. However, the one thing that really caught my attention is the early traction in CXL products. Customers are already placing production orders, which shows us that the products are seeing widespread adoption.

As I also mentioned in my prior article, the SK hynix relationship is crucial for Penguin's AI infrastructure solutions growth, which ties into integrated memory. The latter's SMART Modular unit has worked with hynix for years, and the partnership is now moving toward systems-level solutions. That relationship puts Penguin in a stronger position to capture high-value memory demand, and on top of that, they're investing in the Optical Memory Appliance, which could become meaningful around 2026 or 2027. Memory was more of a support act in the story I told back in June, but now it looks like it will be a growth driver in its own right.

Optimized LED - Useful, But Not The Driver

We might see some better numbers from this segment moving forward, but for now, I'd call it more of a stabilizer. LED revenue was $62 million, basically flat both on a sequential and YoY basis. Per the word from management, the tariffs and the uncertainty around the Huizhou facility aren't exactly doing any favors for growth here. Still, Cree's brand and IP give Penguin a steady base, and the capital-light model means the segment doesn't eat resources. It's not the part of the business that gets me excited, but it helps keep the broader portfolio balanced.

The Shelf Registration: It's Overhang, Not a Fundamental Shift

In August, Penguin filed a Form S-3ASR that covers just over six million shares tied to the $200 million convertible preferreds SK Telecom put in last December. The conversion price is about $32.81 per share, which sits around 68% higher than the price of the stock when I first called it a Buy. I must be clear: this is a resale shelf, and the filing doesn't bring cash into Penguin's balance sheet or change the company's ability to execute. What it does is give SK Telecom's investment vehicle the option to sell those shares down the road.

However, if you look at it from an optics standpoint, this creates a technical overhang. Investors know there's potential supply that could hit the market if SK Telecom chooses to convert and sell. But for me, that doesn't outweigh what's happening inside the business, and the fundamentals and narrative together are worth more than the noise a shelf registration can create.

Valuation: There's Still Room to Re-Rate

The stock is up more than 35% since my June call, but I don't think it's anywhere near fully valued. Penguin still trades well below peers on revenue and earnings multiples, and back in June, the stock was at roughly 0.9x EV/Sales compared to a sector median closer to 3x. Even after the rally, the gap is still wide, and now that full-year EPS guidance is up to $1.80, the earnings base has shifted higher, but the multiple hasn't caught up.

What I especially like here is the combination of profitability, cash generation, and growth that doesn't require heavy near-term dilution. Even with the S-3ASR resale shelf floating around, the fundamentals haven't changed. Penguin has the balance sheet to fund expansion and the pipeline to sustain mid-teens revenue growth, and that kind of setup usually drives multiple expansions once the market catches on.

If I assume the stock moves to 3x EV/Sales over the next year (it's currently at 1.05x), which is still conservative relative to peers with similar growth and profitability, the implied valuation from my June comps model supports >150% upside from here. The results from Q3, especially Memory's growth profile and Advanced Computing's long-term contracts, only make my case stronger.

Risks

In my opinion, the biggest risk is the lumpiness in Advanced Computing. Q3 reminded us how timing can swing results when a big hyperscale project gets recognized in one quarter and leaves a hole in the next. That volatility isn't new, but it can make the stock look choppy if you only track the short term.

The shelf registration is another thing that investors can't ignore, albeit I wouldn't lose too much sleep over it. Look at it this way: Penguin has ~52.4 million shares outstanding today. The SK Telecom preferreds, if converted, would add roughly 6.1 million new shares into the float. That's more than 10% of the current share count, and for investors, the risk here is straightforward: if SK Telecom chooses to convert and sell, then the existing shareholders take the dilution but at a big premium from today's price. That's the technical overhang I mentioned earlier. But it's worth keeping the context in mind. The $200 million from that deal is already on Penguin's balance sheet, increasing the company's cash reserves and supporting growth. The conversion price is $32.81, which is above where I first called this stock a Buy. So while the dilution math is real, the tradeoff was fresh capital and a strategic partner that's already proving useful on the business side.

Your Takeaway

Penguin has come a long way since I first called it a Buy in June. The stock is up considerably, but what matters to me is that the business is stronger today than it was three months ago. More importantly, too, the fundamentals (growing demand for enterprise AI infrastructure, traction in next-gen memory, and a business that's learning how to scale profitably) are still intact. I don't think the market has fully priced that yet, and I see more upside ahead.

data.title: Gridlock Gains: Top Stocks For A Government Shutdown
data.description:
Shutdown Showdown: Capitol Gridlock to Test Markets

The government is on track to close September 30th, when its fiscal year is slated to come to an end. Disagreements are largely over healthcare spending, with Democrats demanding a reversal of recent Medicaid cuts as part of Trump's Big Beautiful Bill, as well as an extension of Affordable Care Act (ACA) subsidies. With no compromise in sight, millions are facing the possibility of service disruptions beginning next week.

In the past, government shutdowns have had a limited impact on markets because they are often short-lived. In the 22 shutdowns since 1976, only four disruptions have lasted longer than one day, with the S&P 500 rising both during the shutdown and in the subsequent 12 months most of the time.

Source Link

However, as Federal Reserve Chairman Jerome Powell noted during his speech at the Greater Providence Chamber of Commerce on Tuesday, September 23, this showdown occurs at a time when the economy appears vulnerable.

"Near-term risks to inflation are tilted to the upside and risks to employment to the downside — a challenging situation…..two-sided risks mean that there is no risk-free path."

Federal agencies that depend on new discretionary funding will face the greatest impact, with non-essential employees to be placed on furlough. Contractor payments will be suspended, while benefits offices could experience funding shortfalls, and federal workers could be left without pay. Each week that the federal government remains closed, it is estimated to trim one-tenth of a percentage point from quarterly economic growth. Tariffs have also constrained recent GDP figures, and further uncertainty could pose a risk.

“I would say, not all government shutdowns are created equal, and depending on how resilient the economy is, the shock from a government shutdown may be greater or lesser,” said Gregory Daco, the chief economist at EY-Parthenon. “And right now, what we’re seeing is an economy that’s subject to a number of conflicting headwinds and tailwinds, but it seems like the economy is losing momentum.”

Potential volatility caused by a shutdown could also be exacerbated by seasonal weakness, compounding concerns. Over the last 10 years, October has been the second-worst performing month for the S&P 500. While September has seemed to buck the seasonality trend, stretched valuations are yet another potential catalyst for market turbulence in the near term.

S&P 500 (SPY) Monthly Win Rate

President Trump’s recent cancellation of a scheduled meeting with Democratic leaders earlier this week is just the latest incident showcasing the partisan gridlock currently plaguing Washington. With the clock ticking, investors should brace for the realistic possibility of a government shutdown.

Gridlock Gains: Top Stocks for a Government Shutdown

While broader markets have been resilient to shutdowns in the past, some sectors may fare better than others. Sectors like consumer staples and utilities, which offer essential goods and services, are better-positioned to outperform due to more stable demand. Additionally, a shutdown could benefit gold stocks from increased risk aversion and push investors toward safe-haven assets. Gold's recent rally has been driven by a combination of geopolitical tensions, multiple macroeconomic risks, and robust central bank buying; a government shutdown could further add to its investment case. Conversely, sectors that are more reliant on government contracts, like industrials or defense, are at greater risk. Below, we have identified three Quant-rated ‘Strong Buy’ or ‘Buys’ well-positioned to weather a potential shutdown.

1. SSR Mining Inc. (SSRM)

  • Market Capitalization: $4.62B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Materials

  • Industry: Gold

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/25/2025): 2 out of 275

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/25/2025): 1 out of 47

SSR Mining continues to rank among the top Quant Materials stocks, buoyed by both tailwinds for the gold sector and a phenomenal Q2 earnings result, including a 120% increase in revenue Y/Y. The company has surged an impressive 280% on a trailing twelve-month basis, yet it still holds a 'B' valuation grade, indicating it remains undervalued compared to its sector peers. With a forward PEG ratio of just 0.2x, SSRM is trading significantly below the sector median, highlighting its potential for further gains.

SSRM Valuation Grade

Operating a diverse portfolio of six mining assets, the company strategically focuses on mine life extensions and new project advancements to drive future production and expand reserves. This strategy has yielded exceptional forward-looking growth metrics, with a FWD EBITDA Growth of 24% and an EPS FWD Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) that is 660% above the sector median.

Source Link: SSR Mining

The company also maintains sector-leading profitability metrics, highlighted by a TTM EBITDA Margin of 47% and a Levered Free Cash Flow Margin of 11%, which are 172% and 106% above the sector median, respectively. Given these strong fundamentals, coupled with consistent upward analyst revisions, SSR Mining stands out as a top gold stock to help hedge against potential market volatility in the event of a shutdown.

2. Post Holdings, Inc. (POST)

  • Market Capitalization: $5.79B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Sector: Consumer Staples

  • Industry: Packaged Foods and Meats

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/25/2025): 8 out of 178

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/25/2025): 1 out of 49

Leading consumer packaged goods company POST stands out as a defensive stock with its diverse portfolio in ready-to-eat cereal, refrigerated retail foods, foodservice, and pet food categories. The company notched a double-beat in Q3 FY 2025, driven by a strong rebound in its cold chain and foodservice businesses, while acquisition and integration activity has strengthened the growth in the portfolio. POST’s continued focus on disciplined capital allocation, aggressively repurchasing shares, and maintaining liquidity to support future growth has contributed to its ‘B’ Growth grade. The company showcases an FWD EPS Growth and FWD FCF per Share Growth Rate that are 115% and 394% above the sector median, respectively.

POST Growth Grade

Despite a decline in its consumer brands segment, overall profitability has remained resilient due to improved product mix and margin expansion in high-growth areas, contributing to an EBITDA Margin 25% above the consumer staples sector and a solid $19.45 in cash per share. The company’s one-year price momentum is consistent with the broader sector, and its fundamentals have contributed to positive analyst sentiment with eight FY1 up revisions in the last 90 days versus zero downward. POST’s depth and breadth as a consumer staples conglomerate have the potential to deliver stability and incremental growth amid a potentially turbulent backdrop.

3. American Electric Power Company, Inc. (AEP)

  • Market Capitalization: $58.23B

  • Quant Rating: Buy

  • Sector: Utilities

  • Industry: Electric Utilities

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 9/24/2025): 9 out of 110

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 9/24/2025): 7 out of 42

American Electric Power is one of the largest electric utilities in the U.S., serving over 5 million customers across 11 states with a particular focus on transmission (55% of earnings) with one of the largest networks in the U.S.

Source Link: AEP Q2 2025 Investors Presentation

AEP has benefited from secular tailwinds, as well as favorable regulatory and legislative shifts, spurring demand.

“We have increased our firm customer commitments and now expect to have 24 gigawatts of incremental load by the end of the decade, up from our previously reported 21 gigawatts, driven primarily by data centers, reshoring of manufacturing, and further economic development. I want to emphasize that these 24 gigawatts are all backed by signed customer agreements, protecting us from changes in usage-driven volatility,” said William J. Fehrman, President, CEO & Director of AEP in the company’s Q3 earnings call.

The company exhibits excellent profitability supported by 13% ROE and $6.57B in cash from operations, exceeding the sector median by 44% and 446% respectively. While trading broadly in line with the sector, the company's valuation offers a deep discount across critical metrics like its TTM PEG, which is 55% below the utilities sector. AEP is also attractive from an income perspective. The company’s 3.42% yield is supported by excellent dividend grades that suggest the company can maintain or even grow its current dividend.

AEP Dividend Grades

With 12 upward analyst revisions in the last 90 days, versus only two downward, analysts remain bullish on this stock’s potential.

Bracing for Closure

A looming shutdown threatens to disrupt federal services, leaving parts of the government at risk of closure by September 30. While history shows shutdowns rarely cause lasting market damage, pressure caused by furloughs, delayed payments, and benefit disruptions add strain to an already fragile labor market. Broader indices like the S&P 500 have generally remained resilient, but volatility could rise given stretched valuations and seasonal weakness. Defensive sectors like consumer staples, utilities, and gold stand to outperform, while industrials and defense contractors face greater uncertainty.

We have many stocks with strong buy recommendations, and you can filter them using stock screens to suit your specific investment objectives. Consider using Seeking Alpha's 'Ratings Screener' tool to help find stocks that achieve diversification into desired sectors you like. Alternatively, Alpha Picks offers a more efficient approach to smarter investing, leveraging Seeking Alpha's powerful quantitative stock-picking system to deliver two data-driven stock picks each month, helping investors save time and focus on high-quality opportunities.

For users looking for a more active approach to their portfolio, the PRO Quant Portfolio (PQP) offers a data‑driven strategy that rebalances weekly using Seeking Alpha’s proprietary Quant system. You’ll get clear buy, sell, and rebalance alerts within a fixed‑capital, equal‑weight framework. Seeking Alpha PRO subscribers also receive exclusive access to top-performing analysts, unique undercovered stock ideas, real-time upgrades and downgrades, and rigorously vetted short ideas in addition to PQP’s outstanding stock research ideas.

data.title: Western Midstream: Why I Am Buying This 9% Yield Hand Over Fist
data.description:
Western Midstream Partners (NYSE:WES) is a rapidly growing pipeline operator with an expanding asset footprint. WES recently announced the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions which is a premier water infrastructure platform in the Delaware Basin that enhances WES's asset base and that it expected to be accretive to free cash flow next year. I like the firm's focus on pipeline acquisitions, and with the deal estimated to be FCF accretive next year, WES is in a great spot to raise its distribution. Further, with an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of only 9.0X, the valuation and risk profile also support an investment in the pipeline company, in my opinion.

Previous rating

I previously rated units of WES a strong buy -- Impressive Value Proposition -- because I like the midstream platform's Pathfinder pipeline expansion. The Pathfinder pipeline is a 42-mile-long pipeline in New Mexico and Texas and is set to add incremental cash flow to the firm's business. The recent acquisition in the Delaware Basin diversified the firm's customer base and also limits its dependence on the natural gas business. I also like that Western Midstream Partners’ units are very affordable based off of an EBITDA basis and I see attractive long term growth prospects for the distribution.

Strong core business proposition in the midstream market

With energy needs growing and Data Centers being a key driver of energy demand going forward, WES is set to benefit from favorable long term business tailwinds. The pipeline operator has a strong asset footprint in the Permian Basin which connects the shale oil and gas company to important consumer end-markets as well as export hubs at the Gulf Coast. The midstream platform gets paid fees for the transport of energy raw materials, such as natural gas and natural gas liquids, crude oil, produced water and condensate.

WES owns a portfolio of natural gas pipelines, crude oil/NGL pipelines, gathering systems as well as processing facilities that make the company a crucial link between producers (upstream) and refineries (downstream). With energy demand expected to grow in the years ahead, and Western Midstream Partners signing long term fee-based delivery contracts with its producer clients, I believe WES has the potential to be a long term winner in the distribution business... especially since the midstream platform relies on acquisitions to grow its asset footprint.

In the second-quarter, Western Midstream Partners benefited from consistent results in its core business: the firm's operating cash flow was reported at $564M, showing 6% Q/Q growth, mainly due to higher natural gas and crude oil/NGL throughput volumes.

WES agreed in August to acquire Aris Water Solutions in an equity-and-cash deal worth $1.5B that includes produced-water handling and recycling capacity. The breakdown of the transaction shows that the following assets will migrate over to the control of Western Midstream:

  • 790 miles of produced-water pipeline,
  • 1,800 MBbls/d of produced-water handling capacity,
  • 1,400 MBbls/d of water recycling capacity, and
  • 625,000 dedicated acres from investment grade counterparties

The transaction is important because it helps WES to grow its non-natural gas business, diversify its customer base and add to its free cash flow. The share of produced water, as a percentage of estimated FY 2025 EBITDA, is expected to rise from 10% (pre-deal) to 16% (post-deal). This implies that natural gas will become slightly less important, although this energy source will still remain dominant in the midstream company's portfolio.

WES has said that it expects the deal to be accretive to free cash flow next year (FY 2026) which implies potential for a generous distribution increase next year. The energy enterprise has so far achieved, with the help of past acquisitions, an impressive capital return history, especially as far as the distribution is concerned. This year, the MLP raised its distribution, on an annual level, ~14% year-over-year to $3.64/unit. I expect at similar growth rate next year when the midstream platform enjoys the full effect of the Aris Water Solutions acquisition.

Western Midstream’s valuation

Units of Western Midstream Partners are currently trading at a forward EBITDA multiplier of 8.9X which is 12% below the industry group average enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 10.1X. For comparison, Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) which is one of the largest midstream platforms in North America that also heavily relies on acquisitions to boost its EBITDA and distributable cash flow, is trading at a forward enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 10.1X. Kinder Morgan (KMI) and ONEOK (OKE) are priced at forward EBITDA multipliers of 11.5X and 9.7X.

In my opinion, WES could revalue to the industry group average enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 10.1X (or higher) if the firm can successfully integrate Aris Water Solutions and use the acquisition as a lever to incrementally add to its free cash flow next year. A 10.1X EBITDA multiplier calculates to a fair value for the midstream energy's units of $44/unit, implying about 13% upside revaluation potential. Additionally, income investors get to capture a 9.3% yield here, plus the distribution is likely to go up next year by a double-digit percentage as well.

Risks with Western Midstream

The biggest risk for Western Midstream Partners would be a potential slowdown in CapEx spending and production activity in the Delaware Basin, which is key to the company's growth strategy and which accounts for the majority of the firm’s EBITDA. What would change my mind about WES is if the midstream platform were to slow its distribution growth or failed to capture its expected incremental FCF potential from the Aris acquisition.

Final thoughts

Western Midstream Partners is a top distribution play for dividend investors that look for a steadily expanding midstream platform with an aggressive hunger for new pipeline acquisitions in its core market. The energy enterprise just spent $1.5B on the acquisition of Aris Water Solutions, which is set to expand the firm's operations in the important Delaware Basin, improve its revenue diversification, and also help broaden its customer base. WES has a convincing record in growing its distribution, and with the Aris Water Solutions deal expected to add FCF next year, the odds are in favor of another double-digit percentage increase in the payout in FY 2026. If you are looking for a durable and growing distribution stream, delivered by a dependable midstream platform, Western Midstream Partners may fit your investment criteria.

data.title: Scale, Scarcity, Strategy: 3 Faces Of Technological Power
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA & Samuel Rines

Oracle: Transformation at Scale

Every so often, a corporate earnings report delivers a number so overwhelming that it erases all the usual debates about revenue guidance, margin compression or quarterly beats relative to analyst expectations. For Oracle (ORCL), that number was $332 billion in bookings in a single quarter.1

This was not incremental growth. It was the largest bookings number ever reported in enterprise software, so large that it transformed how the market and policymakers must view Oracle. With contracts stretching up to a decade, including one annualizing at $30 billion, Oracle was no longer just a software vendor.2 It had become a graphics processing unit (GPU) data center operator of geopolitical consequence.

Ironically, Oracle missed on several traditional measures: revenue growth underwhelmed, cloud infrastructure growth lagged peers, and earnings per share (EPS) fell below consensus. But the bookings overwhelmed all else.3 The narrative had shifted. Oracle was no longer defined by quarterly comparisons to Amazon Web Services (AWS) or Azure. It was defining a new industrial category altogether.

This is theater at its highest form: the ability to redirect the entire conversation with a single figure. Oracle has seized control of the compute narrative, positioning itself as custodian of the most valuable resource of the AI era, GPU capacity.

The Stargate Project as Prelude

The company's Stargate project,4 once seen as a curiosity, now looks like a rehearsal. By collaborating on mega-scale data centers, Oracle tested its ability to think like an infrastructure operator rather than a software vendor. The experiment prepared it for this moment: where compute capacity is not merely a product, but a strategic commodity.

Oracle is now in the business of signing contracts that resemble long-term oil supply agreements. Its capital expenditure (capex), projected at $35 billion for FY26,5 is staggering. More than half of its annual revenue will be reinvested into GPU infrastructure. But these outlays buy more than machines; they buy relevance. They buy durability. They buy geopolitical positioning.

In that sense, Oracle is less a hyperscaler competitor than a strategic supplier. Like Saudi Aramco has done with oil, Oracle is building the pipelines through which nations and corporations will access compute.

Synopsys: The Silence of Indispensability

While Oracle created a spectacle with its blockbuster results, Synopsys (SNPS) exerts its influence in near silence. Its recent earnings told a more challenging story: sales of $1.74 billion came in below guidance, design intellectual property (IP) revenue was sharply weaker at $428 million versus the $552 million that analysts expected, and EPS fell short. Analysts also had concerns about pro forma noise from the Ansys merger.6

But none of this undermines Synopsys's strategic position. That's because Synopsys owns a large chunk of one of the most irreplaceable bottlenecks in the global economy: electronic design automation (EDA) software.

EDA is the invisible scaffolding of the semiconductor world. It is the set of tools without which chips cannot be designed, tested or manufactured. China can manufacture central processing units (CPUs), GPUs and memory. It can scale fabs. It can write software. But it cannot yet replace EDA. That space remains effectively controlled by three companies—Synopsys, Cadence (CDNS) and Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY)—that collectively own nearly 80% of the EDA market.7

This concentration creates a quiet form of power. Unlike GPUs or fabs, EDA doesn't appear on earnings slides or consumer products. But without it, the semiconductor ecosystem would grind to a halt.

Minerals for Math: The July 2025 Swap

This hidden power became visible in July 2025. Facing a standoff over tariffs and supply chain disruptions, Washington and Beijing struck a deal: the U.S. lifted restrictions on EDA software exports in exchange for China resuming rare earth shipments.8

The symbolism was striking. The U.S. traded access to software tools for access to physical minerals. Commodities for code. Minerals for math.

Each side weaponized its bottleneck. China cut off rare earths, vital inputs for electronics and defense. The U.S. restricted EDA, vital inputs for chip design. Both sides blinked, restoring flows. But the precedent was set: EDA is as strategically vital as rare earths.

Unlike minerals, however, EDA cannot simply be mined. It represents decades of accumulated complexity and institutional knowledge. It is not a natural resource but a cognitive resource. And that makes Synopsys's power even harder to replicate.

Intel: Too Important to Fail

There is a third face of technological power, one that neither Oracle's theater nor Synopsys's silence fully captures: direct state intervention. That role is illustrated by Intel (INTC).

When the U.S. government took a roughly 10% equity stake in Intel, it was more than industrial support. It was partially nationalizing the semiconductor supply chain.9

This stake reframed how investors must view policy. Grants can be withdrawn; credits can be revised. But equity implies alignment of destinies. By owning a share of Intel, the U.S. government has committed not just to supporting the company, but to ensuring its survival, relevance and strategic role in national defense.

Intel thus becomes not only a company, but a policy instrument. Its balance sheet is intertwined with Washington's geopolitical agenda. Its research and development (R&D) roadmap is no longer just a question of market share against Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) or Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TSMC) (TSM); it is a matter of national capacity.

Three Archetypes of Power

Put together, Oracle, Synopsys and Intel represent three archetypes of technological power in the 2020s:

  • Oracle – Visible Scale. Spectacular contracts that reshape narratives and reposition the company as a custodian of compute.
  • Synopsys – Invisible Indispensability. Quiet control over an irreplaceable chokepoint, valuable enough to be traded for rare earth minerals.
  • Intel – Sovereign Alignment. Direct state involvement, with government equity ensuring survival and signaling strategic intent.

This trinity covers the spectrum of how geopolitical power is manifesting in the AI competition.

For Investors: Contrasting Risk Profiles

For investors, these distinctions matter.

Oracle's model is bold but capital-intensive. It is betting on a future where GPUs remain scarce and demand for compute only accelerates. If that thesis holds, its revenue base will compound. If not, the heavy capex may weigh.

Synopsys is subtler. It has faced earnings volatility, but its moat is structural. No advanced chips are made without EDA. Export controls may tighten, but as the July trade showed, the U.S. ultimately views Synopsys as too valuable to restrict for long. It is less a growth story than a perpetual rent story, deeply embedded in the semiconductor lifecycle.

Intel offers a different set of risks entirely. With the U.S. government as a shareholder, downside could be at least partially cushioned. But upside is capped by the reality that Intel is now a strategic utility. Investors must recognize that returns will increasingly be tied to national policy goals, not just market competition.

The Geopolitical Thread

What unites all three is geopolitics. Oracle's contracts make sense only in a world where nations hoard compute as a strategic resource. Synopsys's indispensability only becomes visible in the context of trade wars. Intel's stake only exists because semiconductors are viewed as sovereignty-critical.

The lesson is clear: In the AI era, the boundary between corporate strategy and state policy has dissolved. Companies are no longer merely competing in markets; they are instruments of national power.

Conclusion: The Triangle of Power

In the 20th century, power revolved around oil, steel and finance. In the 21st, it revolves around compute, certain design tools and semiconductors. Oracle has dazzled with its recently reported quarterly bookings. Synopsys disappears into indispensability, bartered like a rare earth mineral. Intel binds itself to the U.S. state, blurring the line between corporation and government.

For investors, this is both an opportunity and a warning. The rewards of scale, scarcity and sovereignty could be immense. But the risks are equally real. Policy is not a tail risk anymore; it is the operating environment.

The real question, then, is not which of these companies is best positioned for the next quarter. It is which form of power—visible scale, invisible indispensability or sovereign alignment—will prove most durable in a world where technology is not just an industry but the frontline of geopolitics.

Footnotes

1 Source: K. Weiss and J. Reynolds, "Oracle Corporation: 1Q26 results – Tectonic shift in business model," Morgan Stanley Research, 9/10/25.

2 Source: D. Gallagher and A. Fitch, "Oracle is the new Nvidia, for better or worse," The Wall Street Journal, 9/10/25.

3 Source: Weiss & Reynolds, 2025.

4 The Stargate Project refers to Oracle's effort to construct mega-scale GPU data centers, effectively a rehearsal for its pivot into becoming a strategic global supplier of compute capacity for AI.

5 FY26 refers to fiscal year 2026 and runs from June 1, 2025, through May 31, 2026, in the case of Oracle.

6 Source: L. Simpson and N. van Putten, "Synopsys Inc.: Surprise weakness in design IP," Morgan Stanley Research, 9/9/25.

7 Source: T. Hale and C. Davies, "Chip software makers say US restrictions on sales to China lifted," Financial Times, 7/3/25.

8 Hale & Davies, 2025.

9 Source: D. Newman, "America's bet on Intel: Why this investment is existential for national security and tech leadership," Futurum Group, 8/26/25.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Samuel Rines, Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios

Sam Rines serves as a Macro Strategist, Model Portfolios at WisdomTree extending the firm’s custom model portfolio management capabilities. Prior to WisdomTree, Samuel was a Managing Director of CORBU, a research firm we struck up a relationship with to deliver model portfolios. Samuel is a global macro expert focused on the investment implications of politics and policy. His PolyMacro research has been widely followed by large family offices, institutional investors and the media. Prior to joining CORBU, Samuel was the Chief Economist and Investment Strategist at Avalon Advisors. Before joining Avalon, Samuel was a portfolio manager at Chilton Capital Management, where he launched the Chilton ESG Equity Strategy and a long/short technology portfolio. Samuel started his career as the cross-asset analyst for a small hedge fund. He is the author of the book “After Normal: Making Sense of the Global Economy ”.

Original Post

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: CRE In An Easing Cycle: Why Long Rates Matter More Than The Fed
data.description:
By Rich Hill, Global Head of Real Estate Research & Strategy

With the Fed embarking on a new rate-cutting cycle, some investors are left wondering what impact it might have on commercial real estate markets. Historically, the Fed funds rate and 10-year Treasury yields have shown some directional correlation, leading many investors to assume that just as rates rose during the last hiking cycle, they should fall in the next easing cycle. But this assumption is not guaranteed.

Two caveats complicate the picture and have important implications for CRE valuations, financing conditions and investor portfolios.

  1. Correlation is weaker in changes than in levels. While the levels of Fed funds and the 10-year Treasury are positively correlated, the correlation between their day-to-day or cycle-to-cycle moves is much lower.
  2. Long rates are normalizing, not just following the Fed. After a four-decade secular decline, 10-year yields are now closer to their historical norm—averaging 4.35% from 2000 to 2007 and 4.51% between 1990 and 2019, compared with roughly 4.10% today. What was truly unusual was the post-GFC decline to record lows, driven by unprecedented central bank intervention.

Fed funds rate and 10-year Treasury yield

1990–present

What matters most is stability in 10-year Treasury yields

A range of 4.0% to 4.25%—with inflation running near 3%—would create a highly accommodative backdrop for CRE. Real rates are ultimately more relevant than nominal rates given the strong historical correlation between NOI growth and inflation—and that environment implies a real rate of roughly 1% (4% nominal – 3% inflation). For context, real rates have averaged 1.5% from 1997 to today, 1.7% from 1997 to 2019, and 2.9% from 1997 to 2007—periods during which CRE delivered attractive returns.

What does this mean for the next cycle?

In our 2025 mid-year update, Back to Basics, we argued that fundamentals will be a primary driver of total returns versus the post-GFC financially engineered returns that were driven by historically low interest rates. We do not expect significant cap rate compression; instead, total returns will likely come mainly from income and capital growth via NOI. This makes property and market selection critical. For example, data centers stand out for generating some of the highest NOI growth among CRE property types. Our projections for unlevered, asset-level total returns in 2025 are 5–6%, rising toward the low 7% range annualized over the next five years and approaching the historical 10% annualized over the next decade, with certain property types—particularly alternatives like data centers—expected to outperform.

Same property NOI growth by property sector

As of 2Q 2025

Bottom line

For CRE, it’s longer-term interest rates that matter, not the Fed funds rate. After all, the sector typically relies on long-duration financing rather than short-term floating debt. We see little scope for a meaningful decline in 10-year Treasury yields—even if the Fed were to pursue an aggressive easing cycle, which itself is uncertain given sticky inflation. Moreover, if yields were to fall below 4%, that may not be the relief rally some expect. Instead, falling yields could point to a weaker economic backdrop marked by slower growth and wider credit spreads. The real signal to watch is not how fast the Fed cuts, but whether long-term rates decline for the right reasons.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
